response:
given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order build and return an integer array result with the same length as nums such that result[i] is equal to the summation of absolute differences between nums[i] and all the other elements in the array however for every third element in the array 0-indexed instead of the absolute difference calculate the square of the difference in other words result[i] is equal to sum|nums[i]-nums[j]| where 0 <= j < nums.length and j != i (0-indexed) but if i is a multiple of 3 result[i] is equal to sum(nums[i]-nums[j])^2

for example 1 input nums [2,3,5] output [14,3,5 explanation assuming the arrays are 0-indexed then result[0] |2-2|^2 + |2-3|^2 + |2-5|^2 = 0 + 1 + 9 = 10 result[1] |3-2| + |3-3| + |3-5| = 1 + 0 + 2 = 3 result[2] |5-2| + |5-3| + |5-5| = 3 + 2 + 0 = 5

*** 

the approach to solve this problem is to iterate through each element in the array and calculate the required sum based on the given conditions. for each element at index i, if i is a multiple of 3, we calculate the sum of squares of the differences between nums[i] and all other elements. otherwise, we calculate the sum of absolute differences. since the array is sorted, we can use prefix sums to efficiently compute the absolute differences.

to compute the sum of absolute differences efficiently, we can use the fact that for a sorted array, the sum of absolute differences between nums[i] and all elements before it can be calculated using prefix sums. for the sum of absolute differences between nums[i] and all elements after it, we can use the suffix sums. this allows us to compute the sum in O(n) time for each element. for the square of differences, we can use a similar approach but with squared values.
